summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically recommends the second edition of Steve Krug's 'Don't Make Me Think,' a book on usability and UI design. He considers it the single most important book on usability that anyone should read, noting its accessibility even for non-technical readers like his wife. The post expresses mild frustration that details about what's new in the second edition aren't readily available. Atwood plans to buy the new edition and give away his old copy to spread usability awareness among colleagues.
key_points: Don't Make Me Think is the definitive single-book recommendation for learning usability and UI design
The book is accessible enough for non-developers to enjoy and learn from
Spreading usability knowledge to managers and fellow developers is valuable but getting them to actually read is the challenge
New editions of foundational books are worth keeping up with
money_quotes: If you only read one book on usability and UI, it should be Krug's Don't Make Me Think.
I even gave it to my wife to read, and she enjoyed it too. Is there any higher praise?
Now if only I could get them to read it...
key_insight: Great usability resources are only valuable if you can actually get people to engage with them.
